headlineSummary: Microsoft Copilot drew clearly negative sentiment, while OpenAI stayed neutral on limited evidence and Google Gemini and Anthropic landed in a close tie with mixed-to-slightly positive reactions [E1] [E4] [E5] [E6] [E7].

ranking.summary: Copilot was criticized for ad-like behavior, a strategy complaint, and advice not to use it at all, giving it a clearly negative tone [E1] [E2] [E3].
OpenAI had a neutral reading on limited evidence, with one comment pairing a limitation on asking ChatGPT with praise for vulnerability-finding ability [E6].
Google Gemini drew a small positive note from a user calling Gemini 3.1 Fast really useful for iterative work, but the evidence base is limited [E5].

Anthropic sat in a close tie with Google Gemini, with praise for Claude's capability offset by a neutral mention and a critique that its models mostly pattern-matched rather than doing real code analysis [E4] [E6] [E7].

evidence.excerpt: Copilot edited an ad into my PR

annotations.rationale: The title directly presents Copilot as inserting advertising into a pull request, which is strong criticism of the product's behavior.

evidence.excerpt: GitHub copilot was released in May 2025, so basically it has had an ad since beginning.

annotations.rationale: The commenter says GitHub Copilot has effectively contained ads from the start, criticizing the product strategy.

evidence.excerpt: We should not be using Copilot in the first place.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly argues against using Copilot at all, in the context of data collection and training terms.

evidence.excerpt: Claude was able to basically learn the language from scratch and write those fully compilable Shortcuts for me [1] because it was mind boggling to me that an LLM can do that.

annotations.rationale: This strongly praises Claude's capability, saying it learned a language from scratch and produced fully compilable output.

evidence.excerpt: I have been using this library for a few months alongside Gemini 3.1 Fast It's really useful to get an iteration loop going with an LLM.

annotations.rationale: The commenter explicitly describes Gemini 3.1 Fast as really useful for iterative work.

evidence.excerpt: You can't just ask Claude or ChatGPT to do the binex for you, but even last year's models were really good at finding heap or stack vulns this way.

annotations.rationale: The excerpt mixes a limitation ('can't just ask Claude') with praise that the models were really good at finding certain vulnerabilities.
The excerpt mixes a limitation for ChatGPT with praise that the models were really good at finding certain vulnerabilities.

evidence.excerpt: I read Anthropic's write-up of their recent 0-day hunt that most of this post seems to be based on, and I can't help but notice that (assuming the documented cases were the most "spectacular") their current models mostly "pattern-matched" their ways towards the exploits; in all documented cases, the actual code analysis failed

annotations.rationale: This explicitly criticizes Anthropic's current models as mostly pattern-matching and failing at actual code analysis in the cited cases.
